sections.paragraph:
m. with suff. תִּלִּי (from the root תָּלַל), a hill Jos 11:13, especially a heap of ruins, Deu 13:17 Jos 8:28 Jerem. 49:2 . Hence come the following names of Babylonian cities, so called from hills or mounds near them (see Assemani Bibl. Orient.; Ind. Geogr. t. iii. 2, p. 784; Burckhardt’s Travels in Syria, i. 253, sqq.).

(1) תֵּל אָבִיב (“hill of ears of corn”), [Tel-abib], Eze 3:15, in Mesopotamia, by the river Chebar, perhaps Thal-labba in d’Anville’s Map, L’Euphrate et le Tigre.

(2) תֵּל חַרְשָׁא (“hill of the wood” see חֶרֶשׁ ) [Tel-harsa, Tel-haresha], in Babylonia, Ezr 2:59 Neh 7:61.

(3) תֵּל מֶלַח (“hill of salt”) [Tel-mela], ibid. Ezr 2:59 Neh 7:61.
